title: What Buyers Find in Due Diligence When Sellers Haven't Prepared
linkedin:
Most sellers don't realise the negotiating balance has already shifted by the time a buyer asks for the documents.

At that point, the buyer's team is working from a structured checklist. The seller is running an urgent filing exercise on a timeline set by someone else's interest. The gap between those two positions shows up in the outcome, and not in the seller's favour.

The specific costs are usually quieter than a single dramatic reversal. A price adjustment in return for an undocumented add-back. An earnout clause because the buyer is uncertain whether client relationships will survive the transition. A delayed settlement while missing financial records are reconstructed. Each one is value the seller didn't collect.

The alternative is straightforward: prepare the information before any buyer conversation starts.

A vendor due diligence pack, assembled before the business goes to market, contains the normalised financial history with auditable adjustments, a customer revenue and retention analysis, a summary of key contracts, and an operational overview showing how the business runs without the owner. The buyer still conducts their own due diligence, but the process moves faster and the seller's version of events is already established before the buyer's interpretation can take hold.

The post-EOFY period is the natural window for this work. Twelve months of complete data are available, a sale is still far enough away that the preparation can be done properly, and the vendor due diligence pack built now becomes the foundation for a much better-positioned sale process when the time comes.

If a sale is anywhere in the next two to four years, the documentation work is worth starting before the urgency arrives.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

facebook:
Something worth knowing if you're thinking about selling your business in the next few years: the due diligence process is where most deals quietly lose value.

Not through a dramatic moment. Through small adjustments that happen when a buyer's team has questions and the answers take weeks to find.

A vendor due diligence pack, built before any buyer conversation starts, changes what that process looks like from the seller's side.

If a sale is on your radar in the next three to five years, has the documentation preparation started yet?

google:
For business owners thinking about selling their business in the next few years, due diligence is often where the outcome is shaped more than in any price negotiation.

Most sellers encounter due diligence when a buyer requests documents. By that point, the buyer's team has a structured checklist and the seller is assembling records under time pressure. The gap between a prepared and unprepared seller tends to show up in earnout clauses, price adjustments, and extended timelines.

The solution is vendor due diligence: preparing your own information pack before going to market. A well-built pack contains a normalised financial history with documented adjustments, a customer revenue analysis, key contract summaries, and an operational overview showing how the business runs day to day. The post-EOFY period is the natural moment to build it, when twelve months of complete data are available and a planned sale is still far enough away to do the work properly.

ProfitPulse works with business owners across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria who are preparing for a future sale and want to build that foundation while there is still time. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
